meta_views.view: Partial — login-gated content
meta_views.detail: The public portion of this writeup is a summary/teaser only. The full thesis detail, financial modeling, and specific catalysts cited by Baker at Sohn Montreal are behind a paywall and were not available for extraction. All theses above are inferred from the public summary language only.

theses.summary: No AI bubble today — GPU utilization vs. dark fiber is the key contrast to 2000
theses.detail: At the peak of the 2000 telecom bubble, 97% of fiber laid was dark (unused). Today there are no dark GPUs — all are being utilized at capacity, often to the point of melting during training runs. Nvidia trades at ~40x trailing earnings vs. Cisco's 150-180x at the 2000 peak. Hyperscalers' ROIC has increased ~10 points since ramping capex, confirming positive ROI on AI spend thus far.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states 'I do not believe we're in an AI bubble today' and provides multiple quantitative comparisons; frames it as unambiguous rather than a debate
theses.quote_fragment: there are no dark GPUs

theses.summary: Hyperscaler capex ROI has been strongly positive; debate is whether it continues with Blackwell ramp
theses.detail: The biggest GPU spenders (all public hyperscalers) have seen ~10-point increases in ROIC since ramping AI capex. Baker personally believes ROI will remain positive through the Blackwell buildout, though he acknowledges it is an open debate. Collectively these companies generate ~$300B free cash flow/year and hold ~$500B cash, providing an ~$800B buffer.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'there's no debate that thus far the ROI on AI has been really positive' and personally believes it will continue
theses.quote_fragment: 10-point increase in their ROICs

theses.summary: Nvidia's biggest competitive threat is Google's TPU, not AMD, Broadcom, or Intel
theses.detail: Baker explicitly states Nvidia's primary competitor is Google via the TPU — currently the only viable alternative for training and arguably the best inference alternative. Google is also a problematic competitor because it owns DeepMind/Gemini, which Baker believes is the leading AI company today with 15-20 points of traffic share gain in 2-3 months. Anthropic (Amazon/Google captive) will run on TPUs and Trainium. Only XAI and OpenAI remain as meaningful Nvidia-dependent frontier labs.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states this explicitly and structures his entire chip competitive analysis around it
theses.quote_fragment: Nvidia's biggest competitor... it's Google

theses.summary: Most custom ASIC programs will fail within 3 years; Google TPU external sales could be the catalyst
theses.detail: Baker believes most hyperscaler ASIC programs will be cancelled, especially if Google begins selling TPUs externally (widely rumored). It took Google three generations to get the TPU working — replicating that is extremely difficult. Broadcom is going to market with Meta and others offering custom ASICs plus Ethernet fabric as an alternative to Nvidia's NVLink/InfiniBand stack, but Baker is skeptical of success. AMD will persist as a necessary second-source.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I personally believe most of those ASICs are going to fail' and gives a specific 3-year timeframe
theses.quote_fragment: most of those ASICs are going to fail

theses.summary: Reasoning/RL post-training creates a user-base flywheel that structurally advantages large incumbent AI labs
theses.detail: Pre-reasoning, frontier models without unique data and internet-scale distribution were 'the fastest depreciating asset in history.' Reinforcement learning during post-training means a large user base now unlocks a product-improvement flywheel similar to great consumer internet companies. This structurally advantages Anthropic, XAI, OpenAI, and Google — companies with large existing user bases — and makes it harder for new entrants to catch up.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as a fundamental structural change, not speculative
theses.quote_fragment: reasoning really changed that... flywheel

theses.summary: Application SaaS companies must accept gross margin compression to succeed in AI — avoiding it is an existential mistake
theses.detail: Baker argues it is 'definitionally impossible' to succeed in AI without gross margin pressure due to compute intensity and scaling laws. He draws the parallel to Microsoft/Adobe's successful cloud transition (lower margins but great stocks) and warns that SaaS companies trying to preserve 80-90% gross margins are making the same mistake retailers made ignoring Amazon's low-margin model. Lower gross margins from AI adoption should be seen as a badge of success, not shame.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker uses strong language ('definitionally impossible') and frames it as a clear strategic imperative
theses.quote_fragment: definitionally impossible... to succeed in AI without gross margin pressure

theses.summary: Legacy SaaS companies with profitable existing businesses should fund AI products at breakeven to compete — few are doing this
theses.detail: Baker is surprised more public SaaS companies haven't used their profitable legacy businesses to cross-subsidize AI product development at breakeven. He specifically calls out public coding companies for not competing with Cursor, noting Cursor is accumulating a trillion tokens of coding data that will become a durable moat. The window to compete is still open but closing.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker expresses surprise and frames it as an opportunity, but acknowledges Martin (in audience) is skeptical and calls it 'a chance' not a certainty
theses.quote_fragment: why are none of the public coding companies even trying to compete with cursor

theses.summary: AI frontier model gross margins will be structurally lower than SaaS/internet era margins indefinitely
theses.detail: Due to scaling laws and the importance of test-time compute, frontier AI labs will have structurally lower gross margins than traditional SaaS (80-90%) for the foreseeable future. Baker does not see scaling laws or test-time compute dynamics changing. Lower opex may partially offset this but gross margins will remain fundamentally different.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states this as structural fact tied to scaling laws, not a cyclical view
theses.quote_fragment: gross margins are fundamentally different and until scaling laws change

theses.summary: AI could be a sustaining innovation for Mag 7 incumbents rather than a disruptive one — they have data, compute, distribution, and capital
theses.detail: Unlike the internet which was disruptive to incumbents, AI's raw ingredients (data, compute capital, distribution) are all held in abundance by today's largest tech companies. If they execute well, AI could be a sustaining innovation for many Mag 7 members. Failure to execute risks an IBM-like fate. Google in particular had a 'Pearl Harbor' moment with ChatGPT and is now responding seriously.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a 'reasonable argument' rather than a conviction call, explicitly noting humility is required
theses.quote_fragment: AI could be a sustaining innovation

theses.summary: Google's Chrome and existing user base make AI browser launches by OpenAI/Perplexity strategically risky
theses.detail: Google has ~5 billion Chrome users and could easily launch an AI browser but held back due to regulatory concerns. Now that OpenAI and Perplexity have launched AI browsers, Google has cover to respond — and will likely do so more effectively given Chrome's distribution. Baker implies the AI browser launches by non-Google players may be strategically misguided.

theses.summary: Robotics will be Tesla vs. Chinese competitors — humanoid form factor debate is over
theses.detail: Baker believes robotics is very real and will mirror the Tesla vs. China dynamic in EVs. He believes the humanoid vs. non-humanoid debate is settled in favor of humanoids because they can learn from YouTube videos and humans can demonstrate tasks via motion capture suits. Optimus videos are impressing roboticists.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'very real' and 'that debate is over' on humanoids, but gives no specific investment recommendation or timeline
theses.quote_fragment: Tesla versus the Chinese... that debate is over

theses.summary: Nvidia's roundtripping investments in AI labs are strategically rational competitive responses to Google's TPU ecosystem
theses.detail: Nvidia's investments in OpenAI and other labs (the 'roundtripping' concern) are driven by competitive necessity — Google funds Anthropic with chips and capital, so Nvidia must respond similarly to retain XAI and OpenAI as customers. Baker dismisses roundtripping concerns as overblown and frames all of Nvidia's moves as completely rational long-term strategy. Jensen Huang is one of the two best CEOs Baker has ever known.

theses.quote_fragment: roundtripping concerns are pretty overblown

theses.detail: Baker is emphatic that GPT-5 is a smaller, more economical model designed for cost efficiency for OpenAI/Microsoft, not a frontier capability model. Any inference about scaling laws from GPT-5's performance is invalid. Scaling laws remain intact.

theses.detail: Baker expects AI assistants to negotiate on behalf of users (e.g., hotel bookings) and earn affiliate fees for closed-loop outcomes. AI closing the loop will squeeze out that inefficiency. XAI/Grok is his personal preferred AI assistant given his XAI shareholding.

theses.quote_fragment: affiliate fees... being paid for an outcome

theses.detail: The GB300 is drop-in compatible with GB200 racks (no new power infrastructure needed), meaning rapid deployment. Companies using GB300s—especially vertically integrated ones—will become the new lowest-cost token producers, ending Google's temporary advantage and forcing a change in Google's strategic calculus around negative-margin pricing.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'pretty profound implications' and frames it as a strategic inflection point that will change Google's behavior and impact its stock
theses.quote_fragment: companies that use the GB300s are going to be the low-cost producer of tokens

theses.summary: XAI will release the first Blackwell-trained model in early 2026, giving it a significant competitive advantage
theses.detail: Jensen Huang has publicly stated no one builds data centers faster than Elon. XAI's speed of deployment means they will be first to train on Blackwell and first to use Blackwell for inference at scale. Even after receiving Blackwells, it takes 6-9 months to optimize performance, so XAI's head start is meaningful.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker cites Jensen on record, provides specific timeline (early 2026), and calls it 'an important moment' for XAI
theses.quote_fragment: I think the first Blackwell model will come from XAI

theses.summary: Rubin (next Nvidia GPU after Blackwell) will significantly widen the gap between Nvidia GPUs and TPUs/other ASICs
theses.detail: Baker argues that conservative design choices by Google on TPU v8/v9, combined with the Broadcom partnership friction and MediaTek introduction as a warning shot, mean TPU development is slowing relative to Nvidia's accelerating annual cadence. Rubin will expand this gap significantly.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'the gap is going to expand significantly versus TPUs' when Rubin comes out, but acknowledges uncertainty about TPU v8/v9 specifics
theses.quote_fragment: when Rubin comes out, the gap is going to expand significantly versus TPUs

theses.summary: Google will eventually bring TPU design fully in-house (eliminating Broadcom), making this inevitable on economic grounds
theses.detail: Baker calculates that if TPU revenue reaches $30B by 2027, Google pays Broadcom ~$15B at 50-55% gross margins. Broadcom's entire semiconductor opex is ~$5B, so Google could hire all of Broadcom's semiconductor team at double pay and still save $5B+. Google is already sending warning shots by bringing in MediaTek. This is economically inevitable regardless of what companies say publicly.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says it's 'absolutely inevitable' from first principles and economics, but frames it as a future event with uncertain timing
theses.quote_fragment: the economics make it absolutely inevitable

theses.summary: Most ASICs other than Trainium and TPU will fail; only those with multi-generation experience will be competitive
theses.detail: Baker argues it takes at least three chip generations to make a competitive ASIC (TPU wasn't competitive until v3/v4). Amazon's ASIC team is the best non-Google/Nvidia team. Trainium 3 is the first time it's 'okay,' Trainium 4 will probably be good. Most other ASICs will not succeed. Nvidia's response—annual GPU cadence—makes it nearly impossible for ASICs to keep up across the full stack.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'I will be surprised if there are a lot of ASICs other than Trainium and TPU' and provides detailed generational analysis
theses.quote_fragment: I will be surprised if there are a lot of ASICs other than Trainium and TPU

theses.summary: Data centers in space are superior to Earth-based data centers on every first-principles dimension and represent the most important development in the next 3-4 years
theses.detail: Space data centers benefit from: (1) 6x more solar irradiance than Earth (30% more intense sun, 24hr exposure, no battery needed); (2) free cooling via radiator on dark side near absolute zero; (3) faster inter-rack networking via laser through vacuum vs fiber; (4) direct-to-cell connectivity eliminating terrestrial network hops. SpaceX Starship is the only economically viable launch vehicle. Baker sees Tesla/SpaceX/XAI converging around this infrastructure.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls it 'the most important thing that's going to happen in this world in the next 3-4 years' and describes it as superior 'in every way' from first principles
theses.quote_fragment: in every way, data centers in space are superior to data centers on earth

theses.summary: Power scarcity is structurally bullish for the most compute-efficient chips (Nvidia GPUs), making price irrelevant when watts are the binding constraint
theses.detail: When power is the constraint, tokens-per-watt is the only metric that matters. A data center with superior chips generating 25B in revenue beats a cheaper ASIC data center generating 8B, regardless of capex cost. This gives maximum pricing power to the most advanced compute technologies as long as power remains scarce.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'really important to me as an investor' and provides a specific numerical example to illustrate the point
theses.quote_fragment: if watts are the constraint, the TCO of your compute is absolutely irrelevant

theses.summary: Natural gas and solar are the only realistic near-term power solutions for AI data centers; nuclear cannot be built fast enough in the US
theses.detail: Regulatory constraints (NEPA, permitting) make nuclear construction too slow regardless of political will. Natural gas (especially in basins like Abilene, TX) and solar are the practical solutions. Caterpillar has already announced 75% capacity increase in turbines. The power system is beginning to respond.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker states definitively 'we just can't build nuclear fast enough in America' and points to specific geographic activity (Abilene) as evidence the gas/solar thesis is already playing out
theses.quote_fragment: the solutions are natural gas and solar

theses.summary: SaaS companies are making an existential mistake by refusing to embrace AI agent economics at lower gross margins
theses.detail: Baker draws direct parallel to brick-and-mortar retailers refusing to invest in e-commerce due to margin structure concerns. SaaS companies with 70-90% gross margins are refusing to run AI agents at 35-40% gross margins. This is a 'life or death decision' and essentially every SaaS company except Microsoft is failing it. The correct playbook: launch agents at 10-20% take rates using existing customer data, before AI-native competitors do it for them.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls it 'a life or death decision,' says it's 'an absolute guarantee' of failure if they preserve 80% margins, and names specific companies
theses.quote_fragment: this is a life or death decision and essentially everyone except Microsoft is failing it

theses.summary: Reasoning models have created a data flywheel for frontier AI labs that is beginning to generate increasing returns to scale, widening the moat of the top 4 labs
theses.detail: Pre-reasoning AI had no flywheel—models were static after pre-training. With reasoning, verifiable outcomes allow RLVR feedback loops from user interactions. Each lab also has a more advanced internal checkpoint used to train the next model. This creates compounding separation. Chinese open source was a gift to Meta as a bootstrap, but Blackwell will widen the gap between American frontier labs and Chinese open source, making bootstrapping harder.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'really profound' and frames it as a fundamental change in industry dynamics; uses strong language about separation growing
theses.quote_fragment: reasoning fundamentally changed the industry dynamics of frontier labs

theses.summary: OpenAI is a high-cost token producer due to paying margins to third-party compute providers, creating a structural disadvantage
theses.detail: OpenAI pays margins to compute providers who may not be optimal GPU operators, making them a high-cost token producer relative to XAI (owns infrastructure) and Google (TPUs). This explains the $1.4T Stargate spending commitment and 'code red' urgency. They will not fix this per-token cost disadvantage before XAI and Google have Blackwell/GB300 advantages.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker frames this as explanatory of OpenAI's behavior but acknowledges uncertainty about their internal economics
theses.quote_fragment: OpenAI is a high-cost producer of tokens

theses.summary: Anthropic is burning dramatically less cash than OpenAI while growing faster, benefiting from Google/Amazon TPU and Trainium access
theses.detail: Anthropic's relationships with Google (TPUs) and Amazon (Trainium) give it access to the same low-cost compute advantages as Google. Its recent $5B deal with Nvidia signals Dario Amodei understands Blackwell/Rubin will be superior to TPUs and is hedging. Baker gives Anthropic significant credit relative to OpenAI on capital efficiency.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'you have to give Anthropic a lot of credit' and notes the Nvidia deal as strategically significant, but doesn't provide specific financial data
theses.quote_fragment: Anthropic is burning dramatically less cash than OpenAI and growing faster

theses.summary: Edge AI running pruned frontier models on-device is the primary bear case for cloud AI compute demand
theses.detail: Within 3 years, phones (larger/bulkier with more DRAM) will run pruned versions of models like Gemini 5 at 30-60 tokens/second for free. This is Apple's explicit strategy—be a distributor of AI, run it privately on-device, call cloud 'god models' only when needed. If on-device intelligence at ~115 IQ is 'good enough,' demand for cloud inference compute could disappoint.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'by far the most plausible and scariest bear case' other than scaling laws breaking, and attributes it to Apple's stated strategy
theses.quote_fragment: edge AI is by far the most plausible and scariest bear case

theses.summary: TSMC is being too conservative in capacity expansion, acting as a governor on the AI buildout but potentially making a strategic mistake
theses.detail: TSMC is not expanding capacity as fast as customers want due to fear of overbuild (they dismissed Sam Altman's compute demand projections). This is creating a bottleneck. Intel's empty fabs (under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan) will eventually be filled given compute shortages. Baker thinks TSMC is 'in the process of making a mistake' but acknowledges their caution may prevent a bubble.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says TSMC is 'in the process of making a mistake' but also frames their caution as a 'natural governor' that may be beneficial
theses.quote_fragment: Taiwan Semi is in the process of making a mistake

theses.summary: A true DRAM capacity cycle (not seen since the late 1990s) could emerge as a natural governor and risk to AI buildout
theses.detail: Memory is a gating factor for AI. The last true DRAM capacity cycle was in the late 1990s before TSMC smoothed supply. If demand outstrips supply sufficiently, prices could go up by multiples rather than percentages—a qualitatively different regime. Baker flags this as a potential natural governor/risk but doesn't assign high probability.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker raises this as a possibility ('something that could slow all this down') but doesn't commit to a view on probability or timing
theses.quote_fragment: if we get our first true DRAM cycle since the late 90s

theses.summary: Semiconductor venture capital has returned with force and is necessary for Nvidia's annual GPU cadence to be achievable
theses.detail: The entire Blackwell rack ecosystem (thousands of parts, most not made by Nvidia) must accelerate in lockstep with Nvidia's annual cadence. This requires a healthy semiconductor venture ecosystem. Baker's firm has done more semi VC deals in 7 years than the top 10 VCs combined. This ecosystem also puts pressure on public companies to keep up, and creates investment opportunities across the stack (transceivers, lasers, back blades, memory, networking).
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker calls this 'absolutely foundational and necessary for all of this to happen' and provides personal evidence of his firm's activity
theses.quote_fragment: not even Nvidia can do it alone

theses.summary: Tesla, SpaceX, and XAI are converging into a mutually reinforcing competitive ecosystem
theses.detail: XAI provides intelligence for Tesla Optimus robots; Tesla Vision provides perception; SpaceX provides space-based data center infrastructure for XAI/Tesla AI workloads; XAI has built-in customer bases from Tesla and SpaceX for deploying customer support and sales agents. Each entity creates competitive advantage for the others in a compounding flywheel.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker notes Elon publicly stated this convergence and Baker finds it 'interesting' and 'neat'—moderate rather than high conviction as it's more observational
theses.quote_fragment: Tesla, SpaceX, and XAI converging

theses.summary: Nuclear and quantum computing public equities are in bubble territory; the best players are not publicly investable
theses.detail: Public nuclear/fusion/SMR and quantum companies are not the leaders and lack fundamental support. Quantum leaders are Google, IBM, and Honeywell Quantum—not the pure-play public quantum stocks. Quantum supremacy is widely misunderstood (quantum does some calculations classical computers cannot, but does not replace classical computing broadly).
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says 'none of the public ways you can invest in this are really good expressions of this theme' and calls them 'really clear bubbles'
theses.quote_fragment: two really clear bubbles in nuclear and quantum

theses.summary: Fortune 500 companies with strong technology adoption cultures will be early AI beneficiaries; CH Robinson is an early proof point
theses.detail: CH Robinson went from quoting 60% of inbound requests in 15-45 minutes to quoting 100% in seconds using AI, driving a 20% stock gain. Companies with historical cultures of technology experimentation (e.g., best investment banks) will adopt AI earlier and better. Q3 2025 was the first quarter with specific quantitative AI-driven uplift from Fortune 500 companies outside tech.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker cites specific data point (CH Robinson) and frames Q3 as an important inflection, but broader Fortune 500 adoption is still early
theses.quote_fragment: CH Robinson quoting 100% of requests in seconds because of AI

theses.summary: China made a strategic error by restricting Blackwell access; the gap between American frontier labs and Chinese AI will widen significantly
theses.detail: DeepSeek's v3.2 paper implicitly acknowledged compute shortage as a key competitive disadvantage. China forced Chinese open source to use domestic chips (Huawei Ascend), which are inferior to Blackwell. As Blackwell deploys at American labs, the capability gap will widen. China will likely realize this mistake in late 2026 but by then American labs will have compounding advantages. Rare earth leverage is also overstated as alternatives are being developed rapidly.
theses.confidence_evidence: Baker says China 'made a terrible mistake' and provides DeepSeek paper as evidence, but acknowledges geopolitical uncertainty
theses.quote_fragment: the gap between American frontier labs and Chinese open source is going to blow out because of Blackwell

theses.detail: The largest GPU buyers are public companies with audited financials. Their ROIC is higher now than before they ramped GPU spending. Meta's ROIC declined in one quarter when it couldn't make a frontier model—a cautionary data point.

theses.quote_fragment: the ROIC of the big public spenders on GPUs is higher than before they ramped spending

meta_views.topic: How to evaluate AI model quality
meta_views.view: Must use paid top-tier subscriptions ($200/month); free tier is like evaluating a 10-year-old vs a 35-year-old adult
meta_views.detail: Baker argues many famous investors are reaching wrong conclusions about AI capabilities by using free tiers. The paid tiers (Gemini Ultra, Super Grok, etc.) are qualitatively different. Must also follow the ~500-1000 people globally at the cutting edge, primarily on X/Twitter.

meta_views.topic: AI as an investment research tool
meta_views.view: AI is one of the best tools for keeping up with the pace of AI development itself
meta_views.detail: Baker uses AI to process podcasts, discuss interesting segments, and maintain low friction access (hardware button to pull up Grok). Recommends reading everything Andrej Karpathy writes at least three times.

meta_views.view: OpenAI, Google DeepMind (Gemini), Anthropic, and XAI are clearly the four leading labs; all others have failed to compete
meta_views.detail: Meta threw massive money at frontier models and failed (Zuckerberg's prediction was 'as wrong as possible'). Microsoft failed with Inflection AI acquisition. Amazon's Nova models are not top 20. Making a competitive frontier model is much harder than people thought a year ago.

meta_views.topic: Verifiability as the key to AI automation
meta_views.view: Anything that can be verified can be automated with AI via reinforcement learning with verified rewards
meta_views.detail: Karpathy's insight: with software, anything you can specify you can automate; with AI, anything you can verify you can automate. Best applications: accounting (double-entry must balance), sales (did you make the sale?), customer support (did customer escalate?), model-making (does it balance?).

meta_views.topic: AI progress S-curve and usefulness vs intelligence
meta_views.view: AI needs to transition from getting more intelligent to getting more useful; then usefulness must hand off to scientific breakthrough
meta_views.detail: Models are already at a level where non-experts struggle to see differences between frontier models. The ROI S-curve requires a handoff from intelligence to usefulness (consistent task completion, long context, agentic actions) before scientific breakthroughs create whole new industries.

meta_views.view: Economics will increasingly dominate the prisoners' dilemma as Blackwell and Rubin numbers become too large to ignore
meta_views.detail: All major AI players are terrified of slowing down (existential risk if competitors don't). Microsoft 'blinked' for six weeks and regrets it. But with Blackwell and Rubin, the absolute dollar amounts of capex will force more economic discipline on spending decisions.

meta_views.view: Speculative observation that every bottleneck to AI progress has been resolved just in time
meta_views.detail: Nuclear public opinion shifted exactly when AI needed power. Data centers in space emerging as power constraints bind. Rubin will be a seamless transition relative to Blackwell. AMD entering with MI450. Baker finds this pattern 'a little strange' and connects it to Kevin Kelly's 'technium' concept of technology wanting to grow more powerful.

meta_views.topic: Private equity vs VC holding companies for AI-enabled traditional businesses
meta_views.view: VCs setting up holding companies to apply AI to traditional businesses will not beat private equity at their own game
meta_views.detail: Private equity has been doing operational improvement of traditional businesses for 50 years. PE firms are actually going to be good at systematically applying AI. VCs don't have the operational expertise or relationships to compete with PE in this specific strategy.

meta_views.view: Investing is the search for hidden truths; alpha comes from finding truth before others and being right about it
meta_views.detail: Baker's framework: most thorough knowledge of history intersected with most accurate understanding of current events to form a differential opinion on what happens next. Frames the stock market as a pari-mutuel system where the goal is finding mispriced securities.
